subject: TOOL-TELEMETRY silent failure analysis
executive_summary: Telemetry writes (appendLine to JSONL files) are in the critical execution path of smart tools. If telemetry I/O fails, the entire tool operation's result is discarded — the actual work (e.g., file search, bash execution) is lost. The error surfaces only as a typed defect in the tool's output string visible to the AI agent, with zero logging, zero sentinel values, and zero developer-facing alerts. There is no mechanism to distinguish 'tool operation failed' from 'telemetry side-effect failed.'

actual_mechanism.description: There is no literal `Effect.catchAll(() => Effect.void)` wrapping telemetry in tool/tool.ts. The actual silent-failure mechanism is a three-stage chain: (1) telemetry writes are in the critical path before the return statement, (2) `.pipe(Effect.orDie)` converts any error to an uncatchable defect, (3) `TypedResult.wrapExecute` catches the defect and converts it to a 'success' result with typed error metadata. The tool appears to complete, but the real work was discarded.

stage_1.location: All smart tool execute functions (smart-bash.ts:115-116, smart-bun.ts:171-186, smart-find.ts:135-145, smart-git.ts:194-208, smart-grep.ts:162-174, smart-sd.ts:129-141, smart-write.ts:60-61)
stage_1.pattern: yield* fs.ensureDir(logDir); yield* fs.appendLine(jsonlPath, JSON.stringify(entry)); return { title, metadata, output }
stage_1.problem: appendLine is BEFORE the return. If appendLine fails, the return never executes. The tool's real work result is discarded.

stage_2.location: All 26+ tool files end with `.pipe(Effect.orDie)` (e.g., smart-bash.ts:151, smart-find.ts:167, tool/tool.ts:235)
stage_2.problem: Effect.orDie converts typed failures (like FileSystemError from appendLine) to defects. Defects are intended for unrecoverable logic errors, not recoverable I/O errors. This masks the error type.

stage_3.pattern: Effect.catchDefect((defect) => { const typed = fromDefect(toolID, defect); return Effect.succeed({ output: formatOutput(typed), ... }) })
stage_3.problem: wrapExecute catches the defect and returns a SUCCESS effect with error info in the output text. The tool appears to succeed. The AI agent sees output like '[UNEXPECTED] ENOSPC: no space left on device' but cannot distinguish telemetry failure from tool failure.

scenarios.scenario: Disk is full — telemetry JSONL (smart_tool_usage.v1.jsonl or bash_usage.v1.jsonl) cannot be written
scenarios.root_cause: appendLine is in the critical path before return. ensureDir may also fail.
scenarios.expected_behavior: Telemetry failure should be a non-fatal side-effect. The tool should complete normally and the telemetry error should be logged as a warning.
scenarios.actual_behavior: The entire tool execution fails. The tool's real result is discarded. wrapExecute catches the resulting defect and returns a success effect with errorKind: 'unexpected', status: 'failed'. The AI sees: '[UNEXPECTED] ENOSPC: no space left on device, write'. The AI may retry the tool, getting the same failure each time.
scenarios.developer_experience: The developer sees the AI agent reporting that a tool 'failed unexpectedly.' The error message mentions ENOSPC but is classified as 'unexpected' — there's no indication it's a telemetry/side-effect issue rather than a tool logic bug. The developer has to read the raw error message carefully to deduce it's a disk space issue. In the Effect runtime, there is no log entry, no warning, and no health check alert.
scenarios.logs_produced: none — zero logging in tool/tool.ts, smart tools, filesystem.ts, or typed-result.ts

scenarios.scenario: AppFileSystem.Service is not provided (e.g., test environment that doesn't wire the service layer)
scenarios.root_cause: AppFileSystem.Service is yielded at tool definition time (e.g., smart-find.ts line 48: `const fs = yield* AppFileSystem.Service`). This happens inside Tool.define() / wrap() during tool initialization, not at execution time.
scenarios.expected_behavior: If the service is missing, the tool should fail to initialize with a clear error message.
scenarios.actual_behavior: Effect.catchDefect in wrapExecute catches failures from the EXECUTION path (defects raised during execute()). However, the missing service is a REQUIREMENT error (ConfigError), not a failure in the error channel. MISSING SERVICE ERRORS ARE NOT CAUGHT BY catchDefect. The Effect runtime throws a defect at fiber construction time that propagates to the parent fiber. The fiber crashes with a ConfigError about missing @opencode/FileSystem service. This crash may or may not be visible depending on how the parent fiber handles it. In the tool execution flow within session/tools.ts, the EffectBridge.run.promise() call would reject with this error, which the AI SDK's tool execution would catch as a thrown error.
scenarios.developer_experience: In tests: the test fails with a cryptic 'Missing service @opencode/FileSystem' error that points to the tool definition, not the test setup. No indication of WHICH tool failed or WHY the service is missing. In production: tool registry initialization would fail at startup with a service wiring error — all smart tools become unavailable.
scenarios.logs_produced: none at the tool level. The Effect runtime may produce a default error message.

scenarios.scenario: InstanceState.directory falls back to process.cwd() (instance-state.ts line 22) when InstanceRef is not available at tool definition time
scenarios.root_cause: The InstanceState.context fallback returns `{ directory: process.cwd(), ... }`. The comment on line 18-20 explicitly acknowledges this: 'Tools like RigGitTool capture InstanceState.context during Tool.define() which runs at layer-construction time (before any request provides InstanceRef). Return a best-effort fallback so the tool registry can initialise.'
scenarios.expected_behavior: If the instance directory is wrong, telemetry should either fail loudly or write to a predictable location with a clear log message.
scenarios.actual_behavior: The ensureDir creates the directory recursively at {cwd}/docs/json/opencode/sessions/{sessionID}/analytics/. Since process.cwd() is a valid writable path, the write succeeds. Data is recorded to an unexpected location — the developer's current working directory at process start time. The telemetry is not lost, but it's siloed away from the real instance directory. Later analytics queries (analytics.ts lines 59, 70) that read from the instance directory won't find this data.
scenarios.developer_experience: The developer runs analytics and sees incomplete or missing data for sessions that ran while the fallback path was active. There is no warning, no log message indicating the fallback was used, and no mechanism to discover the missing data. The developer would have to know about the fallback mechanism and manually search for JSONL files in process.cwd().

scenarios.scenario: 80 tools fire concurrently, all writing to the same smart_tool_usage.v1.jsonl
scenarios.root_cause: The appendLine implementation (filesystem.ts lines 176-185) uses a per-file Promise chain: `const prev = appendQueues.get(filePath) ?? Promise.resolve(); const next = prev.then(() => NFS.appendFile(...)); appendQueues.set(filePath, next)`.
scenarios.expected_behavior: Writes should be serialized so records are never interleaved.
scenarios.actual_behavior: The serialization IS correct within a single Node.js process. The synchronous JS block (the `try: () => { ... }` callback) runs atomically in the single-threaded event loop. Two concurrent Effect fibers calling appendLine will see the previous promise correctly. Writes are serialized and records are not interleaved. HOWEVER, this serialization only works within a single process. If multiple Node.js processes write to the same file (e.g., multiple instances of the app), there is NO cross-process coordination and records CAN be interleaved or lost.
scenarios.developer_experience: Within a single process: correct behavior, no issues. Cross-process: corrupted JSONL with interleaved lines, causing parse failures in analytics queries. The developer would see JSON parse errors when running analytics, with no indication that cross-process contention caused it.
scenarios.logs_produced: none for successful writes; appendFile failures produce a FileSystemError that follows the chain described in 'disk_full' scenario

scenarios.scenario: Developer needs to detect, diagnose, or alert on any of the above failure modes
scenarios.root_cause: Complete absence of logging, metrics, or sentinel values across the entire telemetry write path.
scenarios.expected_behavior: At minimum: log a warning when telemetry write fails. Ideally: expose a metric counter for telemetry write failures, a health check endpoint that verifies the telemetry file is writable, and sentinel values in the tool output that distinguish 'tool failed' from 'telemetry failed.'
scenarios.actual_behavior: Zero observability. The entire chain has no logging: tool/tool.ts (0 log calls), all smart tool files (0 log calls), filesystem.ts appendLine (0 log calls), filesystem.ts ensureDir (0 log calls), typed-result.ts wrapExecute (0 log calls for defects caught). The plugin/index.ts trigger function logs hook errors (`log.error('plugin hook error', ...)`) but this is for plugin hooks, not tool-internal telemetry. The only developer-facing signal is the opaque error text embedded in the tool output that the AI agent reads.
scenarios.developer_experience: The developer troubleshooting this issue 6 months from now would need to: (1) notice that tool analytics are incomplete, (2) manually find and tail the JSONL files, (3) search the codebase for where appendLine is called, (4) trace the Effect error handling chain through orDie → catchDefect → wrapExecute, (5) realize there's no logging anywhere in this path, (6) add their own logging to debug. Estimated debug time: 45-90 minutes for a developer unfamiliar with the codebase.
scenarios.debuggability_rating: 1/5 — >30 minutes to root-cause without prior knowledge

debug_timeline.minute_0: Developer notices analytics dashboard shows incomplete data for recent sessions
debug_timeline.minute_5: Checks JSONL files on disk — finds them missing or empty
debug_timeline.minute_10: Checks disk space — discovers disk is full
debug_timeline.minute_15: Wonders why no errors were logged. Searches codebase for 'ENOSPC', 'disk full', 'write error' — finds nothing
debug_timeline.minute_20: Searches for console.error / log.error near tool execution — finds plugin hook logging but not tool telemetry logging
debug_timeline.minute_30: Traces the Effect chain: appendLine → orDie → wrapExecute → catchDefect. Realizes errors are converted to typed results
debug_timeline.minute_40: Verifies by reading the tool output from session messages — finds '[UNEXPECTED] ENOSPC' buried in tool result text
debug_timeline.minute_50: Confirms there is zero logging anywhere in the telemetry write path
debug_timeline.minute_60: Files issue or adds logging. Total time invested: 1+ hour

locations: plugin/index.ts line 72: savePluginHealthStore catches all errors with `Effect.ignore` — disk-full failures when persisting crash counts are silently swallowed
plugin/index.ts line 342: plugin config hook failure logged but `.pipe(Effect.ignore)` discards the error — if a config hook fails due to disk I/O, only a log line is produced
plugin/index.ts line 354: plugin dispose hook failure logged but `.pipe(Effect.ignore)` discards the error
plugin/index.ts lines 411-421: persistCrash uses `Effect.catch(() => Effect.void)` — if capabilityRegistry.setCrashCount/setQuarantined fails, no log, no warning
plugin/index.ts lines 423-434: persistSuccess uses `Effect.catch(() => Effect.void)` — same silent swallow
context/packet.ts line 277: `Effect.catchAll(() => Effect.void)` for conflict detection — if file I/O fails during digest computation, the conflict is silently skipped
additional_findings.note: The codebase has a recurring pattern of wrapping I/O side-effects in catchAll/ignore/void without logging. This makes I/O failures invisible.

additional_findings.description: `.pipe(Effect.orDie)` appears in 26+ tool files as the terminal error handler before returning the execute function. This converts ALL typed errors — including recoverable I/O errors — to defects. The only safety net is wrapExecute's catchDefect, which converts the defect back to a 'success' result with opaque error text. The typed error information (FileSystemError, PlatformError, etc.) is lost in the conversion from failure → defect → typed result.

All smart tools place telemetry writes (ensureDir + appendLine) before the return statement in their execute generators. This makes telemetry a hard dependency of tool success. If telemetry fails, the tool fails. The fix is to move telemetry writes to a fire-and-forget fiber (Effect.fork) or use Effect.tap with catchAll to keep telemetry out of the critical path.

conclusion: The TOOL-TELEMETRY failure path is a silent failure cascade: I/O error → Effect.orDie (defect) → catchDefect (typed success) → AI sees opaque error text. There is zero observability at any layer. A developer debugging this 6 months from now would need 45-90 minutes to trace the Effect error handling chain, discovering that errors are intentionally converted to success results with no logging. The debuggability rating is 1/5.
